serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of Black Bean Rice Salad (No Meat) is approximately 250 g, as typically portioned at a Singaporean hawker stall or food court.

nutrient_source.title: Black Bean Rice Salad — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Black Bean Rice Salad (No Meat), cr_id G00010608, with confidence 0.95 as recorded in the input record. All per_100g values are preserved from the HPB source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Black Bean Rice Salad (No Meat). GI estimated at 64 (medium) based on analogous mixed rice dish with legumes from the University of Sydney GI Database. White rice GI is approximately 72; the addition of black beans (GI ~30) and high fat content (44.5g/100g) substantially attenuates glycemic response. Glycemic load per 250g serving calculated as: GL = (64 × 14.75) / 100 ≈ 9.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium at 810 mg/100g, well above the 600 mg/100g threshold; potassium (64 mg/100g) and dietary fibre (0 g/100g) provide no meaningful offset.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; sodium at 810 mg/100g (2025 mg per 250g serving) approaches the entire daily sodium target of 2000 mg and significantly elevates blood pressure risk — request preparation with reduced or eliminated fish sauce and shrimp paste.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high fat content (44.5 g/100g) and very high caloric density (430 kcal/100g) with zero dietary fibre impair insulin sensitivity and metabolic control despite relatively low carbohydrate (5.9 g/100g).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dish in its standard preparation; the extreme fat load (111 g per 250g serving) promotes insulin resistance and weight gain — if consumed, portion size must be strictly limited and oil quantity reduced substantially, with fibre-rich vegetables or whole-grain rice added.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (44.5 g/100g) with estimated saturated fat of 7.5 g/100g, very high sodium (810 mg/100g), and cholesterol (26 mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish; the combination of high saturated fat, extreme sodium, and caloric density (430 kcal/100g) promotes atherogenesis, endothelial dysfunction, and hypertensive vascular damage — preparation modification to reduce oil by at least 70% and eliminate high-sodium condiments is required before this dish is suitable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 810 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g RA threshold; estimated saturated fat (~7.5 g/100g) exceeds the 5 g gate; zero dietary fibre; chilli is a nightshade ingredient.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; sodium promotes tissue oedema, saturated fat triggers TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines, and the nightshade ingredient (chilli) may exacerbate joint inflammation — reduce fish sauce, shrimp paste, and oil substantially before consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Bean Rice Salad (No Meat) is a Singaporean hawker-style rice dish consisting of cooked white rice tossed with black beans, generous quantities of cooking oil, fish sauce, shrimp paste (belacan), chilli, garlic, and shallots, served without any meat protein. The dish is characterised by an intensely savoury, pungent, and slightly spicy flavour profile derived from the fermented condiments, with a soft, oily texture from the high fat content. Nutritionally, this dish is notable for its extremely high fat content (44.5 g/100g) and very high caloric density (430 kcal/100g), driven primarily by the cooking oil and shrimp paste, while carbohydrate content is relatively low at 5.9 g/100g. Sodium is critically elevated at 810 mg/100g due to fish sauce and shrimp paste, making this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. Dietary fibre is absent (0 g/100g), limiting its metabolic benefit. Despite the 'No Meat' designation, the dish is not vegetarian or vegan due to the presence of fish sauce and shrimp paste. The glycemic index is estimated at 64 (medium) based on the mixed rice-legume composition, with the high fat content attenuating the glycemic response. Clinically, this dish poses high concern across hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease categories and should be consumed only in small portions with significant preparation modifications to reduce oil and sodium.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010608) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence for GI component only), but all other data points are from the exact HPB record.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source (0.0 g/100g reported); this is plausible for a preparation dominated by white rice and oil with minimal whole-food fibre sources. saturated_fat_g (7.5 g/100g) is estimated from the total fat composition (44.54 g/100g) based on typical cooking oil and shrimp paste fatty acid profiles as documented in USDA FoodData Central and HPB reference data; the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not available in the HPB Foodo database entry for this specific dish preparation as of 2026. GI value of 64 is an analogous estimate based on mixed rice-legume dish data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in approved sources. The dish name includes a Burmese-script parenthetical (ပုန်းရေကြီးထမင်းနယ် ကြက်ဥကြော်ပါမယ်) in the original input record; this appears to be a preparation note or variant descriptor rather than a standard dish name and has been preserved in the English alias list for retrieval purposes. The dish is not halal-certified due to the presence of shrimp paste (belacan) which may contain non-halal processing additives depending on brand.
